agenda.detail: One-sentence check-in; seated vocal/breath warm-up. Step-Daughter and Mother take up the objects. The director frames tonight: the whole play held in one room, in words — relationships, layers, tempo, comedy and safety — with no staging at all, and names that tonight substitutes the master schedule's 23 July L&S walk-through, whose cue list is walked in Session 5.

Read or trace each through-line continuously across all three acts: the Father explaining the wound, the Step-Daughter exposing it, the Son refusing it, the Mother carrying it. Where does each relationship turn across the arc — Father/Step-Daughter, Father/Son, Mother/Son, Mother/Step-Daughter? Note where a relationship read clearly in a single act but risks getting rolled over when the whole play runs.

Re-place every performer in their layer (Actors-playing-Actors; the Manager who plays a director first, line 1601; the six fixed Characters; Madame Pace the conjured seventh, line 1665, one of the nine). Track the ontological collisions where 'who is more real' flips — the casting scene's failure of representation, the apparition, 'It's taking place now' (Act Two), the two deaths (Act Three). The architecture is the meaning; confirm each actor can name their layer's pressure at every collision.
Rest the voice; water and food.

Walk the whole play's tempo gears in sequence against what Sessions 1–3 marked: Act One's company looseness vs. the Father/Step-Daughter drive; Act Two's procedural brisk vs. exposed solo vs. airless apparition (and the vamp's mid-bar death); Act Three's rupture-tempi around the two climaxes and the protected silence after the shot. Confirm the gears so Session 5 verifies, not discovers, tempo. No result-direction — tempo is read as collision and interruption, not as 'faster/slower'.

Confirm the register company-wide using Player 1's '[quiet now, almost ashamed; not a punchline]' (line 2332), which falls in ACT ONE just before the Father's 'pity' speech (line 2334), as the model line. Walk the comic beats across the whole play and agree where each laugh must curdle from avvertimento to sentimento. "Valentine" — an Act One moment — confirmed as 'a provocation from a wound' (line 1556), not a number: "if it plays as a merely funny number, the danger underneath it has been lost."
Short scheduled breather. Rest the voice.

Walk the protocol's full contact inventory in words (lines 132–148): the 'My passion, sir!' hand-on-shoulder; the veil-lift (Mother controls speed, holds veto, line 134); Madame Pace's hand under the chin; the shop-scene six-inch limit and screen disappearance; the 'Cry out, mother!' beat (line 144); the Mother separating them; the Father/Son grip (line 148). Confirm each is choreographed-and-consented in August, third party present, never improvised; the back room reported never staged (line 114); the gunshot/drowning object work "as object, not as person" (line 122). Reaffirm consent lives with the receiving actor and is revocable, including in performance (lines 207/231; absolute veto, line 122), and the company's own time-out word, check-in and de-role. Reaffirm 'Bounded bodies, unbounded moral force.' Talked through, never staged.

Each actor — including the Mother, the Son and the Players — names one thing about their character's WHOLE-PLAY arc and one thing they need from the floor to do it safely; de-role check-out; set homework; confirm next Thursday is the cue-called integrated read and the hand-off to August.

exercises.name: Through-line trace across the whole play
exercises.how: Take each principal relationship (Father/Step-Daughter, Father/Son, Mother/Son, Mother/Step-Daughter) and read or summarise its key beats in order across all three acts in one continuous pass, marking where it turns and where, in the whole-play flow, it risks being rolled over by louder energy. Seated, words only.
exercises.purpose: Lets the company feel each relationship as one continuous system rather than three separate act-readings, so Session 5's run and the August floor build on connected through-lines (Stanislavski's through-line; Mitchell's relationships).

exercises.name: Name-your-layer-at-every-collision
exercises.how: Going round the table at each major ontological collision (casting scene, apparition, 'It's taking place now' in Act Two, the two deaths in Act Three), each performer states their layer and the pressure it is under at that exact moment — Actor-playing-Actor / Manager-as-tired-director / fixed Character / Madame Pace the conjured seventh. Words only.
exercises.purpose: Makes the metatheatre operative across the whole play (not just named on day one); the layer you inhabit governs every collision, and 'who is more real' is the play's engine (the edition's seven-Character count, lines 1601, 1665).

exercises.name: The contact-inventory walk-through (confirm, never stage)
exercises.how: Seated circle: walk the protocol's full contact inventory (lines 132–148) in order, each performer confirming the function of the moment that touches them and that it is deferred to consented August choreography with a third party present. Reaffirm the back room as reported, the gunshot/drowning as object work, and revocable consent. No reading-into-action, no touch.
exercises.purpose: Confirms shared, spoken ownership of every charged moment's safe handling before the cued read and the floor (IDC Context/Consent; protocol lines 132–148, 201, 207/231), tracking the protocol's real inventory, not an invented triad — and stands in for the master schedule's 23 July L&S walk-through, whose cue list is instead walked in Session 5.

discussion_prompts: Read whole, did each through-line — the Father explaining, the Step-Daughter exposing, the Son refusing, the Mother carrying — stay continuous, or did one get rolled over when the play ran end to end?
At which collisions does 'who is more real' flip across the whole play, and can each of you name your layer's pressure at that exact moment?
Where did the tempo flatten into one mid-tempo across the arc, and where did the collisions we marked in the act sessions still land as real ruptures?
Did the comedy stay 'nervous, not a punchline' all the way through, and does "Valentine" (Act One) read as a wounded provocation rather than a number?
Walking the contact inventory: is our safety agreement strong enough to carry every one of these into consented August choreography — and what does each of you still need to feel safe?
sessions.homework: Re-read your whole part against tonight's relationship, layer and tempo notes, holding your through-line as one continuous line across all three acts. Bring any remaining questions about the charged scenes to the dedicated, choreographed August sessions — not to the floor unprepared (intake channel open meanwhile). Confirm you can name your layer at each major collision. Keep driving toward the 20 August Act One off-book milestone, progressive thereafter.
sessions.outcome: By the end the company has held the whole play in one room in words: each through-line traced continuously across the arc; every performer re-placed in their layer and able to name its pressure at each collision; the tempo-rhythm gears confirmed end to end against Sessions 1–3; the nervous-not-punchline comedy and "Valentine" (Act One) as a wounded provocation confirmed company-wide; and the charged-scene safety frameworks confirmed in words — the protocol's full contact inventory walked, the back room understood as reported, the gunshot/drowning as object work, consent/veto reaffirmed, and every physical/intimacy moment confirmed as deferred to the 20 August staging block. This evening deliberately substituted the master schedule's L&S walk-through with integrated table work; the cue list is walked in Session 5. Nothing was staged.

sessions.title: The cue-called integrated read — and the hand-off to the floor
sessions.focus: A full integrated CUED READ of the whole play with the SM calling cues aloud and the pianist marking tempo — Act Two given equal time to Acts One and Three — to feel relationships, scored cues and tempo-rhythm end to end as one continuous experience, confirming what Sessions 1–4 built and standing in for the master schedule's 23 July L&S walk-through by walking the full cue list here; then a clean hand-off to the 20 August staging block. A short voice-rest is inserted between Act One and Act Two so no continuous read crosses the 80-minute line. Close-out business is kept light, and front-loaded, so the read can be a true full read.
goals: Run the whole play end to end as one integrated, cue-called read — the SM calling every Light & Sound cue aloud from the prompt book, the pianist marking musical cues by tempo — giving Act Two as much time as Acts One and Three (~40 min each), so the company feels the tempo gears, scored cues and collisions across all three acts as one experience, with the full cue list walked here in lieu of the dropped 23 July L&S walk-through.

Confirm, in the full run, the nervous-not-punchline comedy register (Player 1's Act One model line, line 2332, just before the Father's 'pity' speech, line 2334) and "Valentine" (Act One) as a wounded provocation — verifying the register built in Sessions 1 and 4.

Hear relationships and through-lines land across the whole arc with the cues in place — the Father explains / the Step-Daughter exposes / the Son refuses / the Mother carries — and confirm the tempo-rhythm gears verified in Session 4 hold under a cued run.

Re-confirm the safety frameworks before the floor — the company's time-out word, check-in/de-role, consent and veto (lines 207/231; absolute veto, line 122), and that every choreographed-contact moment is choreographed-and-consented in August — kept brief and front-loaded so it does not eat the read.

Hand off cleanly to the 20 August staging block: confirm the staging calendar (20 Aug–1 Nov; summer break 5 & 13 Aug) and the off-book milestone (Act One off-book by 20 August, progressive thereafter).

agenda.activity: Check-in, warm-up, frameworks read-back, objects in place
agenda.detail: Check-in; seated warm-up. Step-Daughter and Mother take up the objects; pianist on hand to mark musical cues by tempo. Move close-out business UP front so the read is unbroken later: a brief frameworks read-back (each person restates one provision of the company's Room & Safety Agreement and the deferral of the protocol's contact moments to August) and a one-line hand-off note on the August calendar. Kept to ~15 min.

agenda.activity: Integrated read — Act One, with cues
agenda.detail: Full company reads Act One straight through (~40 min), seated, SM calling Light & Sound cues aloud (white → amber → red; the wing radio; "Valentine" sung or marked; the absence of sound into Act Two). Hold the tempo gears: company looseness against the Father/Step-Daughter drive. "Valentine" read as 'a provocation from a wound' — earn the laugh, then let it curdle. Confirm the nervous-not-punchline register on Player 1's '[quiet now, almost ashamed; not a punchline]' (line 2332), the Act One model line just before the Father's 'pity' speech.

A short scheduled voice-rest after the unbroken Act One read, BEFORE Act Two, so no continuous reading crosses the 80-minute line (Act One alone runs from 18:15). Water, throat rest, no talk required. This honours the plan's own 80-minute / voice-rest rule on the most vocally sustained evening of the block.

agenda.activity: Integrated read — Act Two, with cues (full time)
agenda.detail: Read Act Two straight through with EQUAL time to the other acts (~40 min), SM calling cues (the shower; Satie; the Weimar vamp and its mid-bar death; the held chord); pianist marks the music by tempo. Keep the procedural brisk so the opening monologue, the apparition and the Mother's cry land against it. Feel the act's full tempo gears — the cue-dense centre of the play gets the time it needs, not 15 minutes.
Rest the voice; water and food.

agenda.activity: Integrated read — Act Three, with cues
agenda.detail: Read Act Three straight through (~30 min), SM calling cues (cello drone; the single sharp piano note; water sound; the gunshot in real silence; Spiegel im Spiegel as the lights go). Protect the silence after the shot — 'let nobody push.' Let the Manager's exit and the last line land without irony. A 2-minute breather mid-act if the voice tires.

Director gives brief, actionable, motivation-based notes on the whole arc: where tempo flattened, where a collision needs sharpening, where a relationship through-line read clearly or got rolled over — confirming the tempo gears verified in Session 4. No result-direction.

Final de-role check-out for the round of readings; the SM confirms the staging calendar (20 Aug–1 Nov; summer break 5 & 13 Aug) and the off-book milestone (Act One off-book by 20 August, progressive thereafter); the director sets the 20 August arrival expectation and thanks the company, closing the table-work block. (Detailed logistics already covered in the front-loaded read-back, so this stays short.)

exercises.name: Cue-called integrated read, acts equally weighted
exercises.how: Read the whole play seated in one sitting (with the mid-evening break and a short inter-act voice-rest) while the SM calls every Light & Sound cue aloud from the prompt book and the pianist marks musical cues by tempo, giving Act Two the same ~40 minutes as Acts One and Three. No stopping for line fixes; notes are saved for after the read.
exercises.purpose: Lets the company feel tempo-rhythm, the collisions and the scored cues as one continuous experience — the closest a table read gets to the shape of the show — without starving the cue-dense Act Two, which is the centre of the play. Verifies, not discovers, the tempo built across Sessions 1–4, and walks the full cue list in lieu of the dropped 23 July L&S walk-through. This is the one whole-act exposure the act-session two-passes deliberately reserved for tonight.

exercises.name: The 'Valentine' curdle in context
exercises.how: Run the Valentine turn inside the full Act One read (sung or marked), with the room having just heard she is 'two months orphaned and lately for sale'; the company notes the exact moment the laugh should curdle from avvertimento to sentimento. (Valentine is read here in its true Act One position, confirming the work done in Session 1.)
exercises.purpose: Confirms Valentine as 'a wounded provocation' not a comic number (line 1556) — "if it plays as a merely funny number, the danger underneath it has been lost" — under the full cued run.

exercises.name: Frameworks read-back (front-loaded)
exercises.how: At the top of the evening, going round the table, each person reads or restates one provision of the company's Room & Safety Agreement and the deferral of all choreographed-contact work to consented August sessions. Kept brief so the integrated read runs unbroken later.
exercises.purpose: Confirms shared, spoken ownership of the safety frameworks before the floor (IDC/O'Brien: agreed and rehearsed before they are ever needed), without stealing time from the read.

discussion_prompts: Across the whole cued run, where did the tempo flatten into one mid-tempo — and where did the collisions land as real ruptures, as we marked them in the act sessions and Session 4?
Did the comedy stay 'nervous, not a punchline' all the way through — turning from avvertimento to sentimento? Where did it tip toward farce?
With the cues in place, did the Mother's silences and fragile lines — placed in Acts One and Two — survive the louder energy when we ran it whole?
What does each of us most need from the August floor to do this safely and well — and is our safety agreement strong enough to carry it?
sessions.homework: Have Act One off-book by 20 August (the floor's first day); memorise the rest progressively across the staging block and sit with your part-notes over the summer break; arrive on 20 August ready to be on your feet. Re-read your scenes against tonight's tempo and relationship notes. Bring any remaining questions for the charged scenes to the dedicated, choreographed August sessions — not to the floor unprepared. Note the summer-break dates (5 & 13 August) and the staging calendar (20 Aug–1 Nov).
sessions.outcome: By the end you have read the whole play through once as an integrated, cue-called run with Act Two given its full weight and a voice-rest protecting the most vocally sustained evening; felt the tempo-rhythm, collisions and scored cues end to end (confirming what Sessions 1–4 built and walking the full cue list in lieu of the dropped L&S walk-through); confirmed the nervous-not-punchline comedy and "Valentine" (Act One) as a wounded provocation under a cued run; re-confirmed the safety frameworks (time-out, check-in/de-role, consent/veto, the protocol's contact moments choreographed-and-consented in August); and handed off to the 20 August staging block with the calendar set, the Act One off-book milestone clear, and the round of readings complete.

sources.title: Six Characters in Search of an Author — director's edition (six_characters_village_players.html)
sources.author: Luigi Pirandello, adapted and directed by Kiarash Jamshidi (Storer translation, reworked)
sources.note: The production's own master document. Source for every bespoke decision quoted in this plan: the Portraits gallery (the four-stage Father arc; the Mother as silent centre with her three silences, four fragile lines and the veil; the Step-Daughter as moral centre, 'the wit is rage', exposure not flirtation; the Son's refusal and mirror speech with the cry on 'horrible', lines 3607–3609; the Manager who 'plays a director first', line 1601, and his Boy-chair pivot; Madame Pace as the conjured seventh Character / her own performer, line 1665, one of the nine; 'Valentine' as a wounded provocation, line 1556, an ACT ONE moment sung as the family arrives, line 1747; Player 3's pronoun left to the director, line 1647); the part-narrative playing-notes (lines 1891, 1990, 2332/2334, 3133, 3566, 3607); the Light & Sound score (the pianist 'fourth Player, in effect', line 1752); 'It's taking place now' as the key to the production (line 3093) and the line itself in ACT TWO (line 3326, before Act III opens at 3353); the two stage objects; the three stripped settings; the nine-performer count (lines 1687–1691). The act boundaries used to keep each two-pass within its own act: Act I ends line 2574, Act II = 2578–3349, Act III = 3353+. Its master-schedule notes (the SEVEN Thursdays, 18 June–30 July, lines 1782–1803) inform the calendar; this plan deliberately compresses them into five July Thursdays, folds the dropped 18/25 June first-read and walk-through into Session 1, substitutes the 23 July L&S walk-through (line 1798) with Session 4's integrated table work and Session 5's cue-walk, keeps 16 July seated under 'table work, not blocking' (line 1803) rather than the looser 'on his feet' note (line 1796), and says where it adapts the edition's own notes.

sources.title: Intimacy and Consent Protocol (scripts/build_intimacy_protocol.py)
sources.author: Kiarash Jamshidi / Village Players
sources.note: The signed standalone contract. Source for 'Bounded bodies, unbounded moral force' (line 201); 'the intensity is in the report, not the display' (line 125); the back room never staged, carried by the Step-Daughter's account (line 114); the absolute veto for the Step-Daughter and Mother (line 122); the gunshot/drowning handled 'as object, not as person' (line 122); the full contact inventory (lines 132–148: 'My passion, sir!' hand-on-shoulder; the veil-lift with the Mother controlling speed, line 134; Madame Pace's hand under the chin; the shop-scene six-inch limit and screen disappearance; the 'Cry out mother' beat, the most demanding after the shop scene, line 144; the Mother separating them; the Father/Son grip, line 148); revocability including in performance (signature, lines 207/231); and the scope rule that 'The SEVEN Thursdays are table work only ... No blocking. No physical work' (line 179) — this plan works five of those seven evenings. The protocol contains NO time-out word, check-in, de-role or intake channel — those are the production's own practices, not protocol clauses. All charged physical/intimacy work is deferred to the 20 August staging block.

sources.title: The two-pass read (neutral first, exaggerated second) — a verified composite, not a single branded method
sources.author: Synthesised from Mitchell, Donnellan, StageMilk/Casting Networks, Stanislavski/Knebel; assembled for this edition by Kiarash Jamshidi
sources.note: The director's signature opening exercise for each act, run on that act's SPINE SCENES (a selected span, not the whole act — so two passes plus two debriefs fit ~55 min, reconciled with Session 5's ~40 min for a single straight act-read; the whole-act exposure is reserved for Session 5's cued read). Honestly attributed as a composite: NO single canonical source packages 'neutral-then-exaggerated' under one name. Its verified lineages: Mitchell's neutral, fact-first table-work and use of neutral language so the actors are not pre-coloured (the rationale for Pass One); the well-attested anti-line-reading principle (Donnellan, The Actor and the Target; Stareable; No Film School) — a line reading produces copied delivery, not discovered behaviour; exaggeration-to-find-range and its failure mode, result-acting/indicating (StageMilk; Casting Networks); 'Same Lines, Different Meanings' (Theatrefolk) and the 'Italian run' speed read (opera origin) as cousins/variants; and the whole arc resting on Stanislavski/Knebel's Active Analysis — 'the best analysis of a play is to take action in the given circumstances.' Each act's pass is scoped to its own text — Valentine in Act One, the keystone in Act Two, the cry-on-horrible/fountain in Act Three — never to a line from another act. The truthful middle is found on the floor in August, not at the table.

sources.title: The Director's Craft: A Handbook for the Theatre
sources.author: Katie Mitchell (Routledge, 2009)
sources.note: The director-led world-building spine: facts and questions, back-history, the world of the play / big ideas, immediate circumstances (the 24 hours before), neutrally-named events, and intentions ('pictures of the future that drive present action'). The use of neutral language so as not to pre-colour the actors is also the rationale behind the neutral pass of the two-pass read. Used to build the shared objective world before interpretation.

sources.title: Stanislavski's system and Active Analysis (units & objectives, super-objective, through-line)
sources.author: Konstantin Stanislavski; transmitted via Maria Knebel (Active Analysis; A Director's Guide to Stanislavsky's Active Analysis, ed. James Thomas) and Jane Baldwin's 'The Knebel technique'
sources.note: Source for units/objectives, super-objective and through-line; the caution that grounds this plan's tightness (late in his career Stanislavski distrusted long seated analysis for opening a gap between mental and physical work); and the principle underneath the two-pass read — 'the best analysis of a play is to take action in the given circumstances,' with Knebel's étude ladder from silence to the playwright's words. The substance is well-sourced; the popular 'twenty years' duration is not asserted. Hence we get up in August.

sources.title: Actioning; Actions: The Actors' Thesaurus; Different Every Night
sources.author: Max Stafford-Clark and Bill Gaskill (Joint Stock / later Out of Joint); Marina Caldarone & Maggie Lloyd-Williams (Nick Hern Books); Mike Alfreds, Different Every Night (Nick Hern Books)
sources.note: The line-level engine: assign one transitive, playable, present-tense verb per thought, fitting 'I ___ you', introduced only after events/intentions are agreed. The technique emerged from the Joint Stock workshop process co-led by Stafford-Clark and Gaskill in the late 1970s, so the cast holds it as a company rehearsal-room method, not one director's invention. Alfreds restates the caution against over-fixing and frames the ensemble freedom the two-pass read drops into. Gives every line a recoverable, repeatable choice — vital for a semi-amateur company over a run.

sources.title: Respect for Acting / A Challenge for the Actor (the questions / six steps)
sources.author: Uta Hagen
sources.note: The actor's private prep checklist (Hagen preferred the later six steps): Who am I? / circumstances (especially 'what just happened') / relationships / what do I want? / obstacle / what do I do to get it? Dovetails with Mitchell's circumstances; used as homework so quiet roles prep as fully as leads.

sources.title: The Actor and the Target
sources.author: Declan Donnellan (Nick Hern Books, 2002/2005)
sources.note: Keeps the work pointed outward: act toward a specific external target and what the character needs from it, rather than introspecting. 'You can never know what you are doing until you first know what you are doing it to.' Direct lines outward to a changing target, not into oneself — the antidote to indicated line-readings and the way the exaggerated pass is brought back toward truth (action and target, not pushed feeling). Redirects any 'I feel...' note into action.

sources.title: Impro: Improvisation and the Theatre (status work)
sources.author: Keith Johnstone (1979)
sources.note: The lens for the company/ensemble scenes in Act Two: status is played not possessed, the see-saw 'I go up and you go down', and play-the-status read-throughs (a point higher, then lower, then alternating). Makes the vain/working/curious Players-and-Manager dynamics legible and tunable, seated.

sources.title: L'Umorismo (On Humor)
sources.author: Luigi Pirandello (1908)
sources.note: The source of the register this production turns on, and the distinction that makes it teachable: the merely comic is the avvertimento del contrario (the bare perception of the opposite — you laugh); umorismo proper is the sentimento del contrario (the reflective feeling that reverses the laugh into pain). The edition's 'earn the laugh, then let it curdle' IS the move from avvertimento to sentimento — and the exaggerated pass of the two-pass read is the diagnostic that exposes, on Act One's 'Valentine' and on Act Two's casting scene and Madame Pace aria especially, why the comedy must be nervous, not funny-show.

sources.title: Practical guides to running the read-through and the table-work period
sources.author: Theatrefolk ('Having an Effective Table Read' and 'Same Lines, Different Meanings'); The Scene; Much Ado About Drama; Stareable and No Film School (on why early line readings lock premature interpretation); Erin Joy Swank (breaks); Equity break standards
sources.note: Source for the room logistics and the two-pass rationale: read for discovery not correction, don't direct from page one, read first/talk second, group work by relationship so no one sits idle, the prompt book in pencil with a key, a real break inside every 80 minutes (here honoured with a 15-minute mid-evening break PLUS a scheduled 5-minute second break in the charged back half, and in Session 5 a short inter-act voice-rest so no continuous read crosses 80 minutes), empowering the room to call breaks, and progressive off-book toward the floor. 'Same Lines, Different Meanings' is the classroom-scale cousin of the two-pass read (neutral, then through emotions, then debrief).
